findings.issue: No edge cases for Scenario G (Understandable Descriptions)
findings.why_matters: Scenario G describes two-layer descriptions across all 4 design skills but lists zero edge cases. What happens when the agent cannot provide a technical justification (e.g., subjective aesthetic choice)? What if the two layers contradict each other? What if the user explicitly asks to skip human-language layer and get only technical details?
findings.fix: Add edge cases section to Scenario G covering: no technical justification available, user preference for technical-only output, descriptions for non-visual properties (spacing, breakpoints)

findings.issue: designer-experience.md corruption/conflict across concurrent projects not addressed
findings.why_matters: designer-experience.md is a shared global file. If a user runs design-retrospective in two projects simultaneously (or in quick succession), both may read-then-write the same file, causing data loss. Unlike taste-profile.md (per-project, single-writer), this file has multiple potential writers.
findings.fix: Add edge case: concurrent writes to designer-experience.md. Mitigation could be append-only format (each retrospective appends a dated entry rather than rewriting sections) or explicit merge strategy.

findings.issue: 5 scenarios touching 5 different SKILL.md files plus 3 new reference files may exceed size M
findings.why_matters: The spec modifies 4 design skills (init, generate, review, retrospective) + code-writing, creates 3 new reference files (taste-profile template, designer-experience, style-profiles), and adds a cross-cutting concern (two-layer descriptions) to all 4 design skills. This is closer to L than M: multiple independent changes across many files with different integration patterns.
findings.fix: Either re-declare size as L, or split into two increments: (1) taste-profile + experience + retrospective writes (core learning loop), (2) style-profiles + two-layer descriptions + code-writing integration (enhancements). Each increment is independently deliverable.

findings.issue: No observability for the learning loop
findings.why_matters: The core value proposition is that the agent 'learns' over 3+ sessions. If taste-profile accumulates incorrectly or experience categorization drifts, there is no way for the user to diagnose what went wrong without manually reading the files. The spec mentions no way to inspect or reset the learning state.
findings.fix: Consider adding a brief mention of how the user can audit learning: e.g., taste-profile.md includes session timestamps and source of each preference, so user can trace why the agent makes certain suggestions.

findings.issue: Scenario D (code-writing integration) is loosely related to the learning/memory theme
findings.why_matters: The core feature is about learning and memory. The code-writing tokens.json integration is a convenience feature that has nothing to do with learning -- it is about applying existing tokens during coding. This adds scope to a feature that is already borderline L-sized.
findings.fix: Consider extracting Scenario D as a separate S-sized feature. If kept, acknowledge in the spec that this is a bonus item that can be deferred if the core learning loop takes longer than expected.

findings.issue: taste-profile.md structure not specified enough for reliable merge
findings.why_matters: The spec says 'latest wins' for conflicting preferences and mentions sections (color, typography, style, boldness level, anti-patterns). But there is no specification of how the agent determines what constitutes a 'conflict' vs an 'addition'. For example, if session 1 records 'prefers serif headings' and session 3 records 'prefers large spacing', are these additive or does session 3 overwrite session 1 entirely?
findings.fix: Clarify merge granularity: latest wins per-section (color preferences overwrite color preferences, but don't affect typography) or per-entry within a section. A sentence in 'Technical Decisions' would suffice.

findings.issue: design-review skill listed for two-layer descriptions but has no user-facing descriptions
findings.why_matters: Acceptance criterion says 'all 4 design skills describe decisions in two layers'. But design-review outputs max 3 short recommendations in format 'found {what} in {file}:{line} -> use {token}'. This is a diagnostic report, not a design decision explanation. Forcing two-layer descriptions here adds no value and clutters a deliberately minimal skill.
findings.fix: Exclude design-review from the two-layer description requirement, or clarify what two-layer means in review context (perhaps: explain WHY the token is better, not just what to replace).

summary: The spec is well-structured and buildable with the current markdown-only stack. Main concerns: the feature is borderline L-sized rather than M (5 scenarios across 5 skills + 3 new files), designer-experience.md concurrent write risk is unaddressed, and Scenario G lacks edge cases. No critical blockers -- all findings are addressable with spec clarifications.
